content.text:
```json
{
  "signature": "Mozart's Thema from K.331 is a model of Classical symmetry: an A section (bars 1–8) that settles comfortably into A major with an imperfect cadence at bar 4 and a full perfect cadence at bar 8, followed by a B section that builds tension through a half cadence at bar 12 before tonicizing the dominant key of E major across bars 13–16, where the theme reappears reharmonized and closes with its own perfect cadence. A compact two-bar coda (bars 17–18) then steers the music home via a IV–V7–I progression that delivers a final, unambiguous perfect cadence in A major. This entire journey — departure, digression, return — unfolds across just 18 bars, with the chromatic voice-leading detail of the diminished seventh chord at bar 15 (beat 4) providing the one moment of genuine harmonic shadow before the resolution.",
  "significance": "That brief sidestep into E major and the fleeting darkness of the diminished seventh are what give this otherwise sunny theme its quiet sense of longing — the music feels like it is reaching for something just out of grasp before finding its way back home, which is why the final return to A major sounds not merely correct but genuinely reassuring.",
  "connection": "Bach's Fugue No. 19 in A major from the Well-Tempered Clavier (BWV 864b) inhabits the same key and shares something of the same luminous, unhurried quality that A major seems to invite from both composers. Where Mozart's theme achieves its effect through balanced phrases and a carefully placed dominant digression, Bach typically works through the accumulation of interlocking voices that gradually illuminate every corner of the key — a different architecture, but a comparable instinct for letting A major speak with clarity and warmth rather than drama. Both pieces suggest that for these composers the key of A major was not a neutral choice but carried a particular expressive character: bright, singing, and precise."
}
```
